summary: Charity Majors argues that organizations should decouple deploys (building, testing, and rolling out code) from releases (changing user experience), because using the deploy process to control what users see is slow, flaky, and unreliable. Feature flags solve this by letting teams deploy code continuously while controlling user-visible changes independently, enabling progressive delivery and safer testing in production.
key_points: Deploys and releases are fundamentally different actions that most organizations wrongly conflate
Feature flags decouple deploys from releases, turning user experience changes from engineering tasks into product/marketing tasks
Feature flags enable progressive delivery, beta testing, and safe testing in production
Combined with observability tools, feature flags become a superpower for comparing behavior across flag states
You don't need a heavyweight SaaS solution to start—even a simple config database table works
money_quotes: Deploying software is a terrible, horrible, no good, very bad way to go about the process of changing user-facing code.

Deploys are an engineering task, but releases can be done by product managers—even marketing teams. Instead of trying to calculate when to begin deploying by working backwards from 10:00 a.m., you simply flip the switch at 10:00 a.m.
Feature flags are a scalpel, where deploys are a chainsaw. Both complement each other, and both have their place.
Everything gets faster, which instills more confidence, which means everything gets faster. It's an accelerating feedback loop at the heart of your sociotechnical system.
Fixing a single character mistake takes the same amount of time as doubling the feature set!
key_insight: Decoupling deploys from releases via feature flags transforms shipping from a high-stakes engineering event into a routine, low-risk operation that non-engineers can control.
